meta.title: Nested Hearth — Tatar Memory Palace Database
meta.purpose: Single source of truth that binds VOCAB + MEDIA + PALACE so every drill (listening, speaking) is generated from one spine. Edit this file in VSCode/Claude Code; the app and tools read from it.

schema.verbs: inf, stem, vstem, pres, cls(C|V), back(bool), en, cond(1sg example), [loan], [note]
schema.stations: id, locus, locus_en, zone, vocab[surface forms], expressions[tt], media[ids], listen{tt,en}, speak{tt,en}, note, [seal{root,en,peg,statue,action,sentence_tt,sentence_en}] — seals are the mnemonic statue/action images ported from Tatar_Lab/palace_seals.json
schema.verbLab: self-contained Verb Lab toolkit merged from the 'Tatar Verb Lab' Claude design — verbs(id,stem,en,pron,soft,ev,vl,p3,kw,sc,[pre],[disp]) drive the conjugator/pegs/flashcards; subjects/times/places/objects/advs/determiners/builderMods/builderAdjs/bodyParts/colors/adjAll/modifiers/emotionAdjs feed the Sentence Builder; drills/journeySteps/chains/verbPairs/qaBank/nouns/cardinals/ordinals/connectives/rulesRef/endingsRef/aboutMe/pastTimeRef/timeTelling/clockRules/freqRef/timeRef/emoRef/PERSONS feed Respond, Nuances, Numbers, and Dictionary. Independent of vocab.verbs (that spine covers the Palace/Cog/Speech Lab tabs) since the two schemas serve different pedagogy (principal-parts reference vs. mnemonic-peg drilling). Extended for exam prep: doubtRef (modal/doubt particles), directionsRef+cardinalDirections+directionPhrases (location & directions), phrasesRef (the 48 curriculum expressions from vocab.expressions, cross-linked not re-invented), curriculumNouns (the 68 curriculum nouns from vocab.nouns, cross-linked).
schema.hearth: rings[{tt,en,note}] — the nested frame Учак → Бижбүләк → Уфа → Башкортостан → Казан; wings are computed in the app (class rooms by zone, use-case rooms = zone 'Use cases', knowledge wing = knowledge.decks); cities{title,intro,list[{tt,en,region,icon,known,peg,line{tt,en},grammar,[bk]}]} — the Road of Cities cards
schema.knowledge: decks[{id,title,icon,facts[{q,a,[tt],[bk],[note]}]}] — general-knowledge decks with quiz generated in-app

meta.harmony_note: back=true takes -са/-га/-ка/-да/-ны (back vowels а о у ы); back=false takes -сә/-гә/-кә/-дә/-не (front ә ө ү е и). Loanwords carry an EXPLICIT flag because the last written vowel can lie (табип -> back).

VERB (фигыль) for the feeling; Мин сине яратам = I love you. Contrast the NOUN мәхәббәт.
Атна-6 homework: Мин ашасам / Без ашасак.

Җылы киенсәгез дә = even if you all dress warmly.

Телевизор эшләсә дә, мин йоклыйм = even if the TV is on, I sleep.
Онытсаң да = even if you forget.
Bashkir infinitive ends in -ыу (барыу) vs Tatar -у (бару) — a reliable pattern across many verbs. Peg: riding a horse into a BAR.
Seal verb at Kul Sharif: KIL → Keel. Кунаклар килде = the guests came.
Seal verb at Söyembikä Tower: Kal-Fuk әби refuses to move. Мин Казанда калдым.
Voiceless т: past китте (not китде). Поезд кайчан китә? = when does the train leave?
Чәй эч! = drink tea. Homonym of эч = belly.
Market verb: акча түләү = to pay money.
Мин Кол Шәрифкә керәм = I enter Kul Sharif (dative of the place entered).
Reciprocal -ш- on сөйләү: to talk WITH someone. Татарча сөйләшәм = I speak (in) Tatar.
Both 'read' and 'study': университетта укыйм = I study at university; китап укыйм = I read a book.
Peg: a JAZZ pianist scribbling the solo — яз!
Peg: you shout 'EIGHT!' — the only thing you say. Рәхмәт әйтү = to thank.
Peg: an OIL LAmp flicks on over the thinker's head. уйлап табу = figure out.
хәтер = memory (the palace's own word!) + -лә = verb it. Peg: the HAT-TAILOR sews memories into your hat lining.
Peg: you can't stop looking at the shiny CAR. Телевизор карау = watch TV.
Peg: everything within EARSHOT — ишет. Voiceless т: ишетте.
Peg: a hunch SEIZes you.
VOICING: тап → таба, future табачакмын. Peg: TAP the ground — treasure!
эз = footprint, track → эзлә = follow the footprints.
Peg: a giant morning YAWN — уян! уянып китү = wake with a start.
Peg: you wear a giant KEY on a chain. киену (get dressed) is its reflexive cousin, already in the spine.
One-letter verb! Peg: the hose sprays YOU — to wash.
Peg: the PRESSURE-cooker hisses. аш пешерү = cook a meal.
баш = head → 'head it' = begin. Contrast башлану (begin by itself, already in the spine): дәрес башлана vs мин дәресне башлыйм.
Peg: BETTER finish it. укып бетерү = finish reading.
Peg: the BET is settled — over. Сөт бетте = the milk ran out.
Peg: the TUK-TUK screeches to a halt.
тукта + causative -т: you make it stop. Автобусны туктат!
Peg: waiting at the door in your COAT. көтеп тору = wait around.
Peg: you CURL up laughing. көлеп җибәрү = burst out laughing.
Peg: a YELL, then tears. елап җибәрү = burst into tears.
GLIDE STEM: елмай → елмаячакмын (й drops before я). Peg: ела (cry) flips into елмай (smile) — the same tears, curved up.
шат = glad (мин бик шат!) + -лан = become glad.
VOICING + SYNCOPE: курык → курка, куркачакмын. Peg: the dog barks 'КУРК!' and you jump.
GLIDE STEM: сөй → сөячәкмен. Сөям җаным-тәнем белән — the poetic verb of love; ярату is the everyday one.
Peg: the bard drizzles SOY-LA sauce on the tale. Contrast сөйләшү (converse WITH someone, reciprocal -ш).
Peg: with a SAW you make a birdhouse — я-SAW.
GLIDE STEM: куй → куячакмын. Peg: you put the KOI in the pond. язып кую = jot down.
Peg: spill the YOGURT and run.
VOICING: чап → чаба, чабачакмын. Peg: the horse CHOPs at full gallop.
Peg: the OSTRICH finally lifts off. очкыч = the flyer = airplane.
Peg: йөз also means 100 (and face!) — swim 100 laps.
Peg: the hide-and-SEEKer jumps out.
VOICING: чык → чыга, чыгачакмын. Peg: CHECK out and walk out. чыгып китү = head out.
Peg: MEN climbing the ladder. Автобуска менү = board the bus (dative!).
Peg: slide down on your TUSH. төш also = dream & noon — three words in one.
таны (recognize) + -ш (each other). Takes белән: Ашот белән таныштым. Танышуыбызга шат!
исән (alive & well) + -ләш = trade 'be-well's — the verb behind Исәнмесез!
сау бул (stay healthy) + -лаш = trade goodbyes — the verb behind Сау булыгыз!
Peg: 'Уй-на!' shout the kids in the yard. Теннис уйнау, курайда уйнау.
Peg: you send a JIBBERing voice message. As an auxiliary = suddenness: көлеп җибәрү.

nouns.note: NOUN (исем) for love; literary/philosophical (чиста мәхәббәт = pure love). Sufi 'ishq — Salauat's 'ishq majazi -> 'ishq haqiqi thread. Contrast the verb ярату.
Tatar seats intense emotion in the heart (йөрәк) — йөрәк сызылу, йөрәккә ут кабу, йөрәк ярылу.
The other seat of emotion — баш түбәгә тию, башны түбән салу, башка сугу.

тел — халыкның хәтере = language is a people's memory

Мең сум = 1000 rubles.

шәһәрдә = in the city (front). Bashkir: ҡала is the everyday word.
Авыл учагы = the village hearth — the innermost ring.
баш (head) + кала (city): Казан — Татарстанның башкаласы.
Body word hiding in a city name: Стәрле-тамак = 'mouth of the Sterlya river'. Тамагым авырта = my throat hurts.

The Sterlitamak shikhans: Торатау, Юрактау, Куштау — тау = mountain in each name.
Capital of Tatarstan. Казанда = in Kazan; Казанга = to Kazan.

Arabic loan; root of Нурлат and the name Нурия.

әнием = my mother. Bashkir uses a different word: әсәй!
әтием = my father. Bashkir: атай.
FRONT harmony despite the look: өйдә = at home, өйгә = homeward.
киләсе атнада = next week; узган атнада = last week.
Two words in one: киләсе айда = next month; тулган ай = full moon.
киләсе елда = next year; быел = бу + ел = this year.

төнлә = at night.

Җәйдә = in summer.

кызым = my daughter.

Older men are politely 'абый': Марат абый.

Күп кеше = many people (singular after numbers/quantity!).
Минем исемем Салауат. Исемегез ничек? = what's your name?
укы (study) + -т (make) + -учы (one who) = one-who-makes-study.
дустанә күршеләр = friendly neighbors.

китап + ханә (house) — same recipe as даруханә, ашханә.

хаста (sick) + ханә = sick-house.
Туган ил = homeland.
Ends in я → back endings: дөньяда = in the world.
диңгезгә таба = toward the sea.
Кабан күле = Kaban Lake, Kazan's legend-lake.
Йөрәк бүлмәсе, Баш бүлмәсе — the palace's own rooms.
өстәл өстендә = on the table — өс (top) hides in both words.
урын (place) + -дык = the sit-place.
тәрәзә янында = by the window.
машина артында = behind the car.
Автобуска менү = board the bus (dative!).
Arabic loan (qalam) — front endings: каләмем = my pen.
Күлмәгем зәңгәр = my shirt is blue (к→г between vowels!).
Two letters, first-exam certainty: су эчәм = I drink water.

Билем авырта = my lower back hurts.
PEG: үпкәләү (to take offense — already in the spine) is literally 'to lung'! Offense sits in the lungs the way love sits in the heart.
Ак калфак, Агыйдел = ак + идел, the White River.

Сары йорт = the yellow house.

Иске Казан = old Kazan. For people use карт.

карт бабай = old grandfather; картая = grows old.

Bashkir: яҡшы (к→ҡ).
Татар теле авырмы? — Юк, җиңел!

Softens requests: бирегез, зинһар = please give.
Поезд кайчан китә? Bashkir: Ҡасан? (к→ҡ, й dropped).
Frozen dative of иртә (morning): 'to the morning'. Иртәгә мин йөгерәчәкмен.
бер+се+көн+гә ≈ 'to the one-after day'. The step past иртәгә.
From кич (evening). Кичә без бергә йөгердек.
бу + көн = 'this day'.
бу + ел — same recipe as бүген.
From килү — 'the coming one'. киләсе атнада = next week, киләсе айда = next month, киләсе елда = next year.
From узу (to pass) — 'the passed one'. узган атнада = last week. Mirror of киләсе.
Чәй һәм чәк-чәк.
Иртәгә укыйм, ләкин берсекөнгә ял итәм.
Bookish twin of ләкин.
Чәй яки сөт?
Мин татарча өйрәнәм, чөнки ул — минем хәтерем.
Sequences a story: иртән йөгерәм, аннары эшлим.
Lit. 'looking at that'. Арыдым, шуңа күрә йокларга ятам.
бик тәмле, бик яхшы.
Тиз йоклап китәм = I fall asleep fast.
Колин белән бергә = together with Colin.
Тагын бер тапкыр = one more time.
Clitic after its word, harmony-matched: бер генә = just one; чәй генә = only tea.
After the bare noun: Ашот белән. Pronouns take genitive: минем белән = with me.
синең өчен = for you.
+ ABLATIVE: эштән соң = after work; дәрестән соң = after class.
+ ABLATIVE: эштән элек = before work; ике ел элек = two years ago.

expressions.en: until, up to, as far as
expressions.note: + DATIVE: өйгә кадәр = up to the house.
+ DATIVE: диңгезгә таба = toward the sea. (Same shape as таба 'finds' — context decides.)
күпер аша = across the bridge. (Same shape as Аша! 'Eat!' — the bridge is not edible.)
урам буйлап = along the street; moving cousin of …буенда.
тыш = exterior: шәһәр тышында = outside the city. Directions: тышына / тышында / тышыннан.
ара = the gap: таулар арасында = between the mountains (the shikhans!).
тирә = surroundings: күл тирәсендә = around the lake.
каршы = facing: банк каршында = across from the bank.
буй = length: Идел буенда, Агыйдел буенда — the Road of Cities lines use it.
урта = middle: бүлмә уртасында = in the middle of the room.
Кем ул? = who is that? Кемнең = whose (genitive).
Бу нәрсә? = what is this? Нәрсә эшлисең? = what are you doing?
Нинди китап? = what kind of book? Answered by adjectives.
Кайда яшисез? — answered with LOCATIVE -да.
Кая барасың? — answered with DATIVE -га.
Кайдан киләсез? — answered with ABLATIVE -дан. кайда/кая/кайдан mirror the three place cases.
Ничек яшисез? = how are you?
Colloquial; fuller form ни өчен.
Lit. 'for what' — өчен the postposition.
Ничә яшь? = how old? Noun stays SINGULAR: ничә китап.
Бу күпме тора? = how much is this?
Кайсы кайсы? = which is which?
Existence & possession: Минем машинам бар = I have a car. Бүлмәдә өстәл бар.
Mirror of бар: Акчам юк = I have no money. Also the plain 'no'. Bashkir: юҡ.
зуррак = bigger, матуррак = prettier. 'Than' = ablative: Казан Уфадан зуррак.
иң зур = the biggest, иң матур шәһәр = the most beautiful city.

media.title: Complex by Design: The Tatars, Language, Identity, and Cultural Memory
media.by: Chulpan Khismatova (Philologist, Behavioral Researcher, Ethnic Tatar — Duke University)
media.note: CLI guest lecture. Thesis: Tatar as a LIVING ARCHIVE of identity, resilience, and cultural memory — what it means to carry a language and, through it, an entire people's history. Directly mirrors the anamnesis / anti-mankurt frame.

media.title: Tatar Empire: Kazan's Muslims and the Making of Imperial Russia
media.note: History of Kazan's Muslim Tatar society and its role in imperial Russia. Context for the Kazan palace loci.

media.title: Becoming Muslim in Imperial Russia: Conversion, Apostasy, and Literacy
media.note: Literacy, conversion, and apostasy among the Volga peoples — language and faith as identity.

media.title: Аларның уң кулы мин! (I am their right hand!)
media.by: Роберт Миңнуллин (Robert Miñnullin), Tatar children's poet
media.note: Family-hearth poem. Each line stacks a relative in the genitive + the additive да/дә ('...too'). Good for the genitive case and family vocabulary.

media.title: Kul Sharif Mosque at night, Kazan Kremlin
media.note: Visual anchor (Locus image) for the Kul Sharif station. White stone, turquoise domes lit against black sky.

media.title: Ufa real-estate flyer (authentic regional text)
media.note: Authentic everyday text from Ufa (TERA agency, Oktyabrya Ave). Use as a reading/listening sample for the Ufa Gate station — bridges to the Bashkir-heritage Ufa palace.

media.title: Рәхмәт! / Исән булыгыз! — closing greeting slide
media.note: Greeting + farewell anchor; Tatar leather-mosaic art (Sahtian, 2010).

palace.name: Nested Hearth — Kazan class layer
palace.route: Walk the stations in the same order every time (Ricci's rule). Bizhbulyak -> Ufa -> Bashkortostan is the outer frame; this layer is the Kazan class palace.

listen.en: I enter Kul Sharif.

speak.en: Hello! I come here.

stations.note: Dative -кә on Кол Шәриф (front). Greet on arrival.

seal.action: The Keel crashes through the air into the mosque courtyard. It has ARRIVED.

listen.en: I stayed in Kazan.

speak.en: Are you staying in Kazan?

stations.note: The leaning tower of the last queen. Past definite калдым; question particle -мы on каласың.

seal.action: She strikes her staff into the leaning bricks. She REFUSES to move. She STAYS.

listen.en: I am going to the store.

speak.en: We are walking on Bauman Street.

stations.note: Kazan's walking street. Dative of motion (кибеткә, front); урамында = possessive + locative. The Kazan Walk tab drills this locus through all cases.

seal.statue: A rowdy patron walking out of a BAR.
seal.action: He walks down the street tossing coins. He is GOING.
seal.sentence_en: I am going to the store.

listen.en: Language is a people's memory.

speak.tt: Мин татар телен өйрәнәм, чөнки ул — минем хәтерем.
speak.en: I study Tatar because it is my memory.

stations.note: Bind Chulpan Khismatova's lecture here. Speaking prompt = your own 'why I study Tatar'.

seal.action: He chants 'ON-LAH' and his eyes glow gold with UNDERSTANDING.

listen.tt: Әнинең дә улы мин — аларның уң кулы мин!
listen.en: I am mother's son too — I am their right hand!

speak.en: I am my mother's son.

stations.note: Genitive -ның/-нең + additive да/дә. Recite a poem line, then build your own.

listen.tt: Йөрәккә ут кабу — мин сине сагынам.
listen.en: The heart catches fire — I miss you.

speak.en: I miss you.

stations.note: All heart-seated feelings live here. Verb ярату vs noun мәхәббәт.

seal.statue: A giant YARD-RAT in a tuxedo.
seal.action: The rat proposes marriage to the Heart with a diamond ring.

listen.tt: Баш түбәгә тию — мин бик шат!
listen.en: Head to the ceiling — I am overjoyed!

speak.en: I feel at ease today.

stations.note: Head-seated idioms + general mood states.

listen.en: I am reading this book.

speak.en: I love history books.

stations.note: Ross and Kefeli live here — Kazan's Muslim history as palace context.

listen.en: I am buying a house in Ufa.

speak.en: I am going to Ufa.

stations.note: Bridge station to the planned Bashkir-heritage Ufa palace; authentic Ufa text as the reading sample.

listen.en: Salavat Yulaev rides on horseback (lit. goes by horse).

speak.en: I ride a horse.

stations.note: Outer-frame Bashkir layer (Bizhbulyak -> Ufa -> Bashkortostan). Compare бару (Tatar) / барыу (Bashkir) — the -у vs -ыу infinitive split shows up across many verb pairs. ат (horse) is a perfect cognate, identical in both languages — the statue itself is Salavat Yulaev on horseback.

listen.en: My head aches.

speak.en: This is my eye.

stations.note: Body vocabulary — the literal seat of the Heart Room and Head Room idioms. Pair with авырту: X-ым авырта = my X aches.

listen.en: Turn right, then go straight.

speak.en: I am going to the left.

stations.note: Motion to a direction takes the dative (уңга, сулга, төньякка). Spatial postpositions (янында, эчендә) take possessive + locative.

listen.en: Perhaps I will go to Ufa tomorrow.

speak.en: I will speak Tatar.

stations.note: Future definite = vstem + -ачак/-әчәк (vowel-stems: -ячак/-ячәк) + endings. Soften certainty with doubt words (бәлки, ихтимал) or the presumptive -дыр.

listen.en: What time does Amir come? — Probably at five.

speak.en: Is Amir coming at five? — He's probably coming.

stations.note: From the Тыр-мер slide: -мы/-ме asks a yes/no question, түгел negates a noun predicate (Биштә түгel), -дыр/-дер softens a statement into a guess. Stack them: Биштәме? Биштә түгел.

listen.en: If you go to the pharmacy, take this medicine.

speak.tt: Даруханәгә барсаң да, мин синең белән барам.
speak.en: Even if you go to the pharmacy, I'll come with you.

stations.note: Pairs the plain conditional (барсаң, бу дару ал) against its concessive twin (барсаң да, ...) — the same stem, opposite logic, just by adding да/дә.

listen.en: If your head hurts, you need to take medicine.

speak.en: If a tooth aches, you need to go to the doctor.

stations.note: Advice worksheet pattern: [body part] + [symptom verb, conditional] + кирәк. Same frame for every ailment — баш авыртса, салкын тисә, ашказаны авыртса, теш сызласа, күз арса, күңел болганса.

listen.en: May my body be healthy.

speak.en: If you don't do sports, you won't be healthy.

stations.note: Тазарырга! / Ябыгырга! — the slide's two bare-infinitive imperatives, body-positive vs. body-critical framing side by side.

listen.tt: Салкын тисә, чәй белән бал эч.
listen.en: If you catch a cold, drink tea with honey.

speak.tt: Кар яуса да, без укырга барабыз.
speak.en: Even if it snows, we go to study.

stations.note: Concessive drill: Августта гына башланса да — even a far-off, narrow condition (only in August) still takes the same -са да pattern.

listen.en: If I try, I work at an American university.

speak.tt: Мин тырышсам, русча өйрәнәчәкмен. Эшлисем килә.
speak.en: If I try, I will learn Russian. I want to work.

stations.note: Stacks the conditional (тырышсам) with the future definite (өйрәнәчәкмен) and the desiderative (эшлисем килә) — three tenses/moods practicing the same ambition.

listen.tt: Әнием рөхсәт итмәсә, мин аны тыңламыйм.
listen.en: If my mother doesn't permit it, I don't listen to her/it.

speak.en: I permit it myself.

stations.note: рөхсәт итү = light-verb compound (permission + 'do'); only итү conjugates, рөхсәт stays fixed.

listen.tt: Дустым туган көненә чакырса, матур бүләк сатып алырга кирәк.
listen.en: If my friend invites me to their birthday, I need to buy a nice gift.

speak.en: I'm buying a gift for my friend.

stations.note: сатып алу = compound verb 'sell-and-take' = buy; only алу conjugates, сатып stays as a fixed converb.

listen.en: If they don't ask, we don't go hiking to the forest.

speak.en: I love walking in the forest.

stations.note: Reunites урман and хайкинг, which already lived in vocab.nouns but had no station of their own.

listen.tt: Рәхим итегез! Чәй эчегез, чәк-чәк ашагыз.
listen.en: Welcome! Drink tea, eat çäk-çäk.

speak.en: Thank you! The tea is very tasty.

stations.note: The hospitality script — the innermost hearth ring made speakable. Plural imperatives: эчегез (front), ашагыз (back). Bashkir: Рәхим итегеҙ.

listen.tt: Бу калфак күпме тора? — Мең сум.
listen.en: How much is this kalfak? — A thousand rubles.

speak.en: I am buying çäk-çäk.

stations.note: Bargaining frame: Бу ... күпме тора? → price in сум → сатып алу (compound 'buy'). Bashkir: Был күпме тора?

listen.tt: Миңа аш һәм икмәк бирегез, зинһар.
listen.en: Please give me soup and bread.

speak.en: I drink milk, I eat soup.

stations.note: Ordering frame: dative миңа + imperative бирегез + зинһар. ашханә = аш (meal) + ханә (house), like даруханә.

listen.tt: Поезд Уфага кайчан китә? — Сәгать биштә.
listen.en: When does the train to Ufa leave? — At five o'clock.

speak.tt: Миңа Уфага бер билет бирегез, зинһар.
speak.en: One ticket to Ufa, please.

stations.note: Travel frame that rides the Ufa Gate bridge. Loanword harmony: билетка, поездга. Bashkir: Өфөгә (Уфа → Өфө).

listen.en: The day after tomorrow we will go to Kazan.

speak.en: Next week I will speak Tatar.

stations.note: This week's room. The timeline: кичә → бүген → иртәгә → берсекөнгә. 'Next' = киләсе (the coming one): киләсе атнада / айда / елда; 'last' = узган. Before/after = ablative + элек/соң: эштән соң.

listen.tt: Иртәгә мин укыйм, ләкин берсекөнгә ял итәчәкмен.
listen.en: Tomorrow I study, but the day after tomorrow I will rest.

speak.tt: Мин арыдым, шуңа күрә йокларга ятам.
speak.en: I got tired, so I am going to bed.

stations.note: The glue room. һәм joins, ләкин/әмма turn, чөнки explains, аннары sequences, шуңа күрә concludes; modifiers tune (бик тиз, бергә, тагын, бер генә). Both drill lines reuse worksheet compounds: ял итү, йокларга яту.

listen.en: Where do you live? When are you coming?

speak.en: What is this? Who is this?

stations.note: Every exam question starts here. The place trio mirrors the cases: кайда (at, -да) · кая (to, -га) · кайдан (from, -дан). ничә + SINGULAR noun: ничә китап, not *китаплар.

listen.en: Today is Monday; tomorrow will be Tuesday.

speak.en: My birthday is in January.

stations.note: Days are Persian-built (шәмбе); җомга is the Arabic mosque-day. Seasons hide pegs: яз (spring) = яз (write!), көз (autumn) is one dot from күз (eye). Months are loans — locative tells the story: гыйнварда, февральдә.

listen.en: My shirt is blue, my car is red.

speak.tt: Казан — зур һәм матур шәһәр. Иң матур!
speak.en: Kazan is a big and beautiful city. The most beautiful!

stations.note: Adjectives never agree — no plural, no case: зур шәһәрләрдә. Compare with -рак + ablative (Казан Уфадан зуррак), crown with иң. Careful homonym: кара black / кара! look!

listen.en: The book lies on the table.

speak.en: In my room there is a big window.

stations.note: The postposition drill-room: everything is somewhere relative to the furniture. Sentence recipe: [place phrase] + [thing] + бар/юк — Өстәл астында сумка юк.

verbs.sc: Stroll into a BAR — to go.
A boat's KEEL glides ashore — to come.
Grab your KIT and leave.
You CALL out: I'll stay!
Step in with CARE — to enter.
A CHICK hatches out of the shell.
UTTERLY tired, you sit down.
The bull TORO rises to its feet.
You yawn, claw the blanket, sleep.
A huge YAWN — you wake up.
You devour A SHAwarma — to eat.
An icy drink: you gasp ETCH!
PRESSURE-cook the stew.
You spray YAZ graffiti on the wall.
OOH-KEY, you read aloud.
A BELL rings — now you know.
The ANGLE clicks — you get it.
You SAIL a tale across the room.
Two boats SAIL & chat the day away.
You shout EIGHT! — to say.
SORRY, may I ask a question?
You hand over a BEER — to give.
Take it ALL.
You watch the KARAte match.
At last you see the CURE.
IS-IT a sound? — you hear it.
Listen close, TONGs to your ear.
You love your gleaming YACHT.
You TELL a wish you want.
SIT UP, take it ALL — to buy.
You SAT and sold the whole lot.
You work as ASH LAYs on the forge.
YA, the SAW builds it — to make.
Y'ALL EAT, then rest a while.
You search behind the EASEL.
You TAP it — found at last!
OH NOT again — you forgot.
A HEADER jogs your memory.
Oy, you RUN the drills — to learn.
You teach the oy-RAT a new trick.
A KEBAB on loop — repeat, repeat.
TARGET-ME, then translate the line.
You wander streets of YORE.
A YOGa-er sprints down the track.
WHOSE lane is this? — you swim.
A BEE dances mid-air.
Belt out JEER-ah — to sing.
Oy now! the kids play on.
You TIRE yourself trying hard.
Pay the TOLL at the booth.
Cha, here! — you invite them in.
Put the SALT in the pot.
Set the COOing dove down gently.
Smile wide and yell MY!
You laugh at the lump of COAL.
You cry out and YELL-a.
Your heart SAGs in longing.
You meet a TANned stranger.
You bump into a joyful RASH of friends.
The BASH kicks off — to begin.
You settle the BET — finished.
You wait by the COT.
Bring the KITE back home.
You use the COOL fAN all summer.
You travel and SAY: a hat!
A curious KIZIK draws your interest.
The KEY fits — you agree.
YOU scrub your hands clean — to wash.
Coach-la! — a big warm hug.
OOP! — a quick kiss on the cheek.
You shout "yeah, sha!" — living it up.
An OIL LAmp flicks on over the thinker's head — to think.
A sudden hunch SEIZes you — to sense.
You wear a giant KEY on a chain — to wear.
The BET is settled, the game is over — to end.
The TUK-TUK screeches to a halt — to stop.
Wave the TUK-TUK down — causative -т stops something else.
шат (glad) + -лан = become glad — to rejoice.
The OSTRICH finally lifts off — to fly. очкыч = airplane.
The hide-and-SEEKer leaps out — to jump.
MEN climbing the ladder — to climb, to board.
Slide down the hill on your TUSH — to descend.
Trading Исәнме!-s at the door — исән (well) + -ләш.
Trading Сау бул!-s at the gate — to say goodbye.
You send a JIBBERing voice message — to send.

drills.en: I read a book in the evening.
He goes to school in the morning.
She listens to music at home.
He doesn’t drink coffee in the morning.
I usually cook food at home.
Tomorrow I will go to the store.

journeySteps.en: In the morning I wake up.
In the evening I talk with friends.

steps.en: In the morning I wake up.
In the evening I talk with friends.

chains.title: Буш вакытта · in my free time

steps.en: In my free time I read.
In the evening I listen to music.

chains.title: Кибеткә · a trip to the store

steps.en: In the morning I wake up happy.
During the day I get tired.
In the evening I rest calmly.

verbPairs.note: уян = your eyes open, you become conscious. тор = you physically rise / stand up. Иртән мин уянам, аннары торам.
кара = look at / watch on purpose (телевизор карыйм). күр = perceive, see — often involuntary: Мин аны күрәм.
бел = hold knowledge of a fact. аңла = grasp meaning. Мин беләм, ләкин аңламыйм — I know, but I don't understand.
әйт = say one specific thing. сөйлә = narrate, speak at length. Add -ш → сөйләш = converse with someone (mutual).
бар = go toward a destination. кит = depart / leave from here. чык = step out of an enclosed space (бүлмәдән чык).
ал = take / get. сатып ал = literally 'take by selling' = buy — same verb ал, with сатып in front.
эшлә = do a task, work (өй эше эшлим). яса = make / produce, and set phrases: йога ясыйм, күнегү ясыйм.
бар = a single trip somewhere. йөр = attend regularly or move about: мәктәпкә йөрим = I go to school (every day).
ярат takes an object: мин кофены яратам. ошый flips it — dative + 3rd person: миңа кофе ошый = coffee pleases me.

emoRef.note: lit. 'my mood [is] good'; кәефем начар = bad mood
арырга — past tense marks the state you're now in

qaBank.en: Did you read a book yesterday?

qaBank.yes: Әйе, мин еш спорт белән шөгыльләнәм.
qaBank.note: Swap the frequency word to soften a 'no': еш → сирәк (rarely).
'Like' here is ошарга — it flips to dative (миңа …) + 3rd person, never -мын.
арырга (to tire) — past tense describes the state you're in right now.
No verb — кәеф (mood) + adjective. Flip yes/no by swapping яхшы ↔ начар.
тор = get up (rise). Note it's not уян (wake up).

qaBank.en: Do you read in your free time?
qaBank.yes: Әйе, мин буш вакытта китап укыйм.

qaBank.en: Did he come to work yesterday?

qaBank.note: Indefinite past (-ган) — used for life experience you're reporting.
ярат takes a direct object (музыканы) — contrast with ошый above.
Soften a 'no' with сирәк (rarely) rather than a flat negative.
бу = this. To say something is NOT a noun, use түгел — not verb negation.

qaBank.en: Is there a book on the table?
qaBank.note: 'There is' = бар, 'there isn't' = юк — both land last.
Possession uses бар/юк: literally 'my car exists / doesn't exist'.
You can answer by flipping the frequency word itself: сирәк ↔ еш.

qaBank.yes: Әйе, миңа татар теле бик кызык.
qaBank.note: кызык (interesting) is an adjective — negate with түгел.

qaBank.en: Did you go abroad last year?
qaBank.yes: Әйе, мин былтыр чит илгә бардым.
qaBank.no: Юк, мин былтыр чит илгә бармадым.
qaBank.note: былтыр = last year → pairs with the definite (witnessed) past -дым.

connectives.note: links words or clauses: чәй һәм кофе
goes after the word; voiceless → та/тә. Ул да килә.
mild contrast: Мин барам, ә ул бармый.

rulesRef.title: Yes / no questions: -мы / -ме
rulesRef.body: Add the question particle to the word you're asking about — usually the verb or predicate. No word-order change. -мы after back-vowel (hard) words, -ме after front-vowel (soft) words.
rulesRef.ex: Син барасың → Син барасыңмы? · Ул өйдә → Ул өйдәме? · Бу китапмы?

rulesRef.title: There is / isn't: бар / юк
rulesRef.body: бар = there is / exists / I have. юк = there isn't. The thing comes first; бар or юк lands last.
rulesRef.ex: Өстәлдә китап бар. · Минем машинам юк.

rulesRef.title: How many: ничә / күпме
rulesRef.body: ничә = how many (countable, expects a number). күпме = how much / how many (amount). The counted noun stays SINGULAR.
rulesRef.ex: Ничә китап? — Биш китап. · Күпме акча?

rulesRef.title: This / that / these / those
rulesRef.body: бу = this, шул = that, теге = that (over there). For 'these / those', keep the word singular and pluralize the noun instead.
rulesRef.ex: бу китап = this book · бу китаплар = these books · шул кеше = that person

rulesRef.title: Indefinite past, 1st person
rulesRef.body: The -ганмын / -гәнмен form (I …) often means you weren't aware of the action — it was accidental, or you can't personally remember it. Contrast the plain definite past -дым (you witnessed it).
rulesRef.ex: Мин онытканмын = I forgot (not on purpose) · Мин 1947 елда туганмын = I was born in 1947

endingsRef.title: -лы / -ле — with / having
endingsRef.body: Turns a noun into an adjective meaning 'having X'. Vowel harmony picks -лы (back) or -ле (front).
endingsRef.ex: көч → көчле (strong) · тәм → тәмле (tasty) · акыл → акыллы (clever)

endingsRef.title: -сыз / -сез — without
endingsRef.body: The opposite — 'lacking X'. -сыз after back vowels, -сез after front.

endingsRef.title: -рак / -рәк — comparative (more)
endingsRef.body: Add to an adjective for 'more / -er'. Front vs back harmony.

endingsRef.title: иң — superlative (most)
endingsRef.body: Just place иң before the adjective — no suffix needed.
endingsRef.ex: иң зур (biggest) · иң яхшы (best) · иң матур (most beautiful)

endingsRef.title: -лар / -ләр — plural
endingsRef.body: Marks plural nouns (spoken variants -лар/-ләр/-нар/-нәр by harmony). Drop it after a number.
endingsRef.ex: китап → китаплар · кеше → кешеләр · but: биш китап (five books)

endingsRef.title: -дагы / -дәге — 'the one at/in'
endingsRef.body: Builds an adjective of place from the locative: 'the … that is at/in X'.
endingsRef.ex: өйдәге китап (the book at home) · шәһәрдәге парк (the park in the city)

aboutMe.en: My name is …, and I live in ….

aboutMe.tat: Мин еш китап укыйм, чөнки тарих белән кызыксынам.
aboutMe.en: I often read, because I'm interested in history.

aboutMe.tat: Мин кофе яки чәй эчәм, ләкин кофены күбрәк яратам.
aboutMe.en: I drink coffee or tea, but I like coffee more.

aboutMe.tat: Мин татарча сөйләшәм, инглизчә дә сөйләшәм.
aboutMe.en: I speak Tatar, and I also speak English.

aboutMe.tat: Кичен мин ардым, шуңа күрә иртә йоклыйм.
aboutMe.en: In the evening I'm tired, so I sleep early.

aboutMe.tat: Буш вакытта мин йөгерәм һәм музыка тыңлыйм.
aboutMe.en: In my free time I run and listen to music.

clockRules.t: Half past → use the NEXT hour
clockRules.b: [next-hour ordinal] + ярты. алтынчы ярты = 5:30 — literally 'half of the sixth'.
[coming hour] тулырга [minutes] минут. җиде тулырга ун минут = 6:50.
Add the locative -да/-дә: сәгать дүрттә = at 4 o'clock.

doubtRef.note: Follows the verb/adjective, marks a guess based on evidence you can see. Ул өйдә бугай = He seems to be home (I think, based on the lights being on).
Similar to бугай but slightly more tentative — 'I suppose'. Ул кайткан ахры = He's probably come back.
Comparison or resemblance, follows a noun or clause. Ул арыган кебек = He looks/seems tired (lit. 'like tired').
Interchangeable with кебек in most contexts, slightly more literary. Яңгыр ява шикелле = It looks like it's raining.
A stronger, more confident guess than бугай/ахры — placed before the clause. Мөгаен, ул онытты = He probably forgot.
Genuine uncertainty, often opens the sentence. Бәлки, мин иртәгә килермен = Maybe I'll come tomorrow (pairs naturally with the aorist future).
'It's possible that...' — can stand with булырга (мөмкин булырга) or alone as a one-word hedge.
Softens a claim toward 'as good as'. Бетте диярлек = It's almost finished.
Marks something as obvious/certain — the opposite pole from бугай/ахры on the confidence scale.

directionsRef.note: Postposition + locative; follows the genitive-marked noun: өйнең эчендә = inside the house.
өстәлнең өстендә = on top of the table.
мәктәпнең янында = next to the school. Most common everyday 'near'.
кибетнең каршында = across from the store.
ике өйнең арасында = between the two houses.
мәктәп алдында = in front of the school.

directionPhrases.note: Мәктәпкә ничек барырга? = How do I get to school?
Imperative plural/polite — command form of бару (to go).
якын = near, түгel negates a noun/adjective (not a verb).
бик = very, modifies the adjective якын.

phrasesRef.en: to be deeply touched by love/affection
to feel defeated, fall into despair
to be utterly shocked by bad news

phrasesRef.note: closing from the CLI / Tatar.Mosaics lecture slide
X кебек = like X / seems like X
киләдер = he's probably coming; өйдәдер = must be home
Attaches (harmony-matched) to the word being questioned: Әмир киләме? = Is Amir coming? Биштәме? = At five?
Mыни/мени — stronger, more surprised cousin of -мы/-ме.

phrasesRef.en: is not, not (negates a non-verbal predicate)
phrasesRef.note: Биштә түгел = Not at five. Әмир түгел = It's not Amir. (Contrast verb negation, which uses -мый/-ми on the verb itself.)
Conditional verb + да/дә, harmony-matched to the verb form: Онытсаң да = even if you forget. Кар яуса да = even if it snows.
Desiderative: verb stem + -асы/-әсе (possessive-marked) + килә. Эшлисем килә = I want to work (lit. 'my wanting-to-work comes').

phrasesRef.en: to feel restless, bored, or anxious

curriculumNouns.note: NOUN (исем) for love; literary/philosophical (чиста мәхәббәт = pure love). Sufi 'ishq — Salauat's 'ishq majazi -> 'ishq haqiqi thread. Contrast the verb ярату.
Tatar seats intense emotion in the heart (йөрәк) — йөрәк сызылу, йөрәккә ут кабу, йөрәк ярылу.
The other seat of emotion — баш түбәгә тию, башны түбән салу, башка сугу.
улы = his/her son (3sg possessive); бабайның улы = grandfather's boy
уң кул = right hand
central to the lecture: тел = the living archive
мәдәни хәтер = cultural memory (lecture theme)
тел — халыкның хәтере = language is a people's memory
LOANWORD HARMONY: back endings (табипка) despite front и
туган көн = birthday
capital of Bashkortostan; gateway to the planned Ufa palace
йөз also = face (and = hundred)
homonym with эч! = drink (imperative)
Даруханәгә барсаң, бу дару ал = if you go to the pharmacy, take this medicine. Dative -гә confirms front harmony despite the back-vowel start.
Ашказаны авыртса = if the stomach aches.
Тәнем сәламәт булсын = may my body be healthy.
Таза бала, таза бәби = healthy child, healthy baby.
Ябык-таза = thin-and-fit.
Сөям җаным тәнем белән = I love with my soul and body.
дустым = my friend (possessive).
русча = in Russian / the Russian language (adverbial form).
Predicate modal, not a regular noun: ... кирәк = need to .... Negative conditional Кирәкмәсә дә = even if it isn't needed (кирәк behaves like a defective C-stem: кирәк+мә+сә).
Cognate — identical in Tatar and Bashkir (ат/ат). Peg: an OTTER riding a horse.
Кунаклар килде = the guests came (Kul Sharif seal sentence).
Чәй табыны = the çäy table — the hospitality script lives here.
The celebration sweet; front endings: чәк-чәкне.
Ашыгыз тәмле булсын! = may your meal be tasty.
Adjective: бик тәмле = very tasty.
Мең сум = 1000 rubles.
Бик кыйммәт! = too expensive! Opposite: арзан.
LOANWORD HARMONY: билетка (back endings despite и/е).
Бауман урамы = Bauman Street; урамында = on the street (poss + loc).
Front endings: кибеткә барам = I go to the store.
Сөембикә манарасы = the leaning Söyembikä Tower.
Сәгать биштә = at five o'clock.
The Kal-Fuk seal peg at Söyembikä Tower — калфак anchors кал (to stay).
Bashkir national instrument; its flower is on the Bashkortostan flag.
шәһәрдә = in the city (front). Bashkir: ҡала is the everyday word.
Авыл учагы = the village hearth — the innermost ring.
баш (head) + кала (city): Казан — Татарстанның башкаласы.
Body word hiding in a city name: Стәрле-тамак = 'mouth of the Sterlya river'. Тамагым авырта = my throat hurts.
Стәрле елгасы = the Sterlya river. Volga = Идел, Kama = Чулман, Belaya = Агыйдел.
LOANWORD: заводта (back). Нурлатта шикәр заводы бар.
шикәр заводы = sugar refinery (Nurlat's landmark).
Чистай's Vostok factory — Командирские watches. Izafet: сәгать заводы.
The Sterlitamak shikhans: Торатау, Юрактау, Куштау — тау = mountain in each name.
Capital of Tatarstan. Казанда = in Kazan; Казанга = to Kazan.
Tatar name of Chistopol, on the Кама (Tatar: Чулман).
Front harmony: Бөгелмәдә = in Bugulma.
From нур = light (Arabic loan): the 'radiant' town.
LOANWORD HARMONY: Иннополиска (back endings).
Стәрле + тамак = mouth of the Sterlya. Bashkir: Стәрлетамаҡ (к→ҡ).
Arabic loan; root of Нурлат and the name Нурия.
Arabic loan; voiceless п: китапка, китапта, китаптан.
Кол Шәриф мәчете = Kul Sharif Mosque (izafet).
әнием = my mother. Bashkir uses a different word: әсәй!
әтием = my father. Bashkir: атай.
FRONT harmony despite the look: өйдә = at home, өйгә = homeward.
киләсе атнада = next week; узган атнада = last week.
Two words in one: киләсе айда = next month; тулган ай = full moon.
киләсе елда = next year; быел = бу + ел = this year.
Frozen dative иртәгә = 'to the morning' = TOMORROW — this week's word.
кичен = in the evening; кичә = yesterday (from the same root).
төнлә = at night.
эштән соң = after work (ablative + соң); root of эшләү.
күпер аша = across the bridge.
ял итү = to rest; ял көне = day off.
ярдәм итү = to help (+ dative: Колинга ярдәм итәм).
сәяхәт итү = to travel.
тәрҗемә итү = to translate.
Телевизор карамаячакмын = I will not watch TV.
Day 1. Persian-derived day names, шәмбе = 'sabbath day'.
пәнҗе = five (Persian) — the fifth day from Saturday.
From Arabic Jumu'ah — the mosque day. Җомгада = on Friday.
якшы + шәмбе ≈ 'the good day'.
HOMONYM: яз! also = write! Peg: in SPRING the JAZZ pianist writes outdoors. Язда = in spring.
Җәйдә = in summer.
One dot from күз (eye): autumn is when the year's EYE closes. Көздә = in autumn.
Voiceless ш: кышта = in winter.
Вакыт юк! = no time! Вакытында = on time (lit. at its time).
Гаиләгез бармы? = do you have a family?
кызым = my daughter.
Older women are politely 'апа': Гөлнара апа.
Older men are politely 'абый': Марат абый.
Tatar splits siblings by AGE, not just gender: апа/абый older, сеңел/эне younger.
Also 'born': туган көн = birth-day, туган тел = native tongue.
Күп кеше = many people (singular after numbers/quantity!).
Минем исемем Салауат. Исемегез ничек? = what's your name?
укы (study) + -т (make) + -учы (one who) = one-who-makes-study.
дустанә күршеләр = friendly neighbors.
Voiceless п: мәктәптә = at school, мәктәпкә = to school.
LOANWORD: университетта, университетка (back).
китап + ханә (house) — same recipe as даруханә, ашханә.
аш + ханә = meal-house.
хаста (sick) + ханә = sick-house.
Туган ил = homeland.
Ends in я → back endings: дөньяда = in the world.
диңгезгә таба = toward the sea.
Кабан күле = Kaban Lake, Kazan's legend-lake.
Йөрәк бүлмәсе, Баш бүлмәсе — the palace's own rooms.
өстәл өстендә = on the table — өс (top) hides in both words.
урын (place) + -дык = the sit-place.
тәрәзә янында = by the window.
машина артында = behind the car.
Автобуска менү = board the bus (dative!).
Arabic loan (qalam) — front endings: каләмем = my pen.
Күлмәгем зәңгәр = my shirt is blue (к→г between vowels!).
Two letters, first-exam certainty: су эчәм = I drink water.
The Tatar staple — бәрәңге белән ит.
Каш белән күз арасында = 'between eyebrow and eye' = in a blink.
Билем авырта = my lower back hurts.
PEG: үпкәләү (to take offense — already in the spine) is literally 'to lung'! Offense sits in the lungs the way love sits in the heart.
Ак калфак, Агыйдел = ак + идел, the White River.

Сары йорт = the yellow house.
Adjectives never agree: зур шәһәр, зур шәһәрләр — no plural on зур.
Яңа ел = New Year.
Иске Казан = old Kazan. For people use карт.
Ничә яшь? = how old? Миңа 21 яшь = I am 21.
карт бабай = old grandfather; картая = grows old.
Танышуыбызга шат, матур Казан! Comparative: матуррак.
Bashkir: яҡшы (к→ҡ).
Татар теле авырмы? — Юк, җиңел!

hearth.title: Нестед учак — The Nested Hearth
hearth.intro: Walk from the fire outward. Each ring is a place you belong to; each wing hangs rooms on that ring. Walk the same route every time (Ricci's rule) and the wheel turns on its own.

rings.note: The family fire at the center. Every route begins and ends here — Рәхим итегез!
The home village ring: family, food, the çäy table. Гаилә учагы and Чәй табыны hang here.
The city over the Belaya (Ағиҙел). Salavat's monument, the Ufa Gate, the train. Уфа → Өфө in Bashkir.
The homeland ring — the kinship layer. The four shifts live here: с↔һ, ч↔с, к↔ҡ, з↔ҙ.
The outermost walkable ring: the class palace, the Kremlin walk, the exam rooms.

cities.title: Шәһәрләр юлы — The Road of Cities
cities.intro: Between the village hearth and the Kazan wing runs a road of real towns. Each is a locus: a peg image, one line of Tatar to say there, and a reason to remember it. Walk them in order: home ring first, Tatarstan wing after.

list.known: The home village — a district center in southwest Bashkortostan, long home to Chuvash, Tatar, and Bashkir neighbors side by side; the innermost ring of this palace.
list.peg: The family учак (hearth) burning in the window — every route starts and ends at this fire.
list.grammar: Ablative of origin: -тән after voiceless к. 'Кайдан сез?' — 'Мин Бижбүләктән.'
list.bk: In Bashkortostan the road signs read Бишбүләк — 'five gifts' (биш бүләк) is the folk etymology.

list.known: Second city of Bashkortostan (founded 1766 as a salt landing on the Belaya). Beside it rise the шиханнар — Torataw, Yuraktaw, Kushtaw — ancient Permian coral reefs turned lone mountains. In 2020 people encircled Kushtaw and saved it from the soda quarries.
list.peg: A giant THROAT (тамак) swallowing a river: Стәрле + тамак = 'mouth of the Sterlya'. A body word hiding inside a city name.
list.grammar: буенда = along/on the bank of (postposition, like Идел буе in the Kazan Walk).
list.bk: Bashkir: Стәрлетамаҡ (к→ҡ); the Belaya is Ағиҙел — 'White Idel'.

list.known: Capital of Tatarstan, millennium city, seat of the whole class wing of this palace — the Kremlin, Kul Sharif, Bauman Street, Kaban Lake.
list.peg: A giant cauldron (казан = cooking pot!) boiling on the Volga bank — the legend says the city is named for one.

line.en: Kazan is the capital of Tatarstan.

list.bk: Bashkir: Ҡазан — the ҡ again. The pot is also a казан in both languages: плов is cooked in one.

list.known: Russia's youngest town — founded in 2012, inaugurated as a city in 2015 — an IT city across the Volga from Kazan, built around Innopolis University. Delivery robots and driverless taxis share its streets.
list.peg: A robot in a түбәтәй delivering чәк-чәк — the newest town on the oldest road.
list.grammar: Loanword harmony: Иннополиста (back -та after voiceless с), plural -лар on робот.

list.known: Old merchant grain port on the Kama (Tatar: Чулман). In WWII the Soviet writers' union was evacuated here — Pasternak lived and worked in Chistopol — and so was the watch factory that became Vostok, maker of the Командирские.
list.peg: A merchant on the riverbank checking an enormous wristwatch while barges of grain float past.
list.grammar: Чулман = the Tatar name of the Kama; same буенда pattern as Sterlitamak — reuse the frame.

list.known: Railway junction town of the southeast oil uplands; home to Tatneft's research institute. In 1918 Jaroslav Hašek — author of Švejk — served as deputy commandant here and later wrote comic tales about it; the town keeps a Hašek museum.
list.peg: The good soldier Švejk saluting an oil derrick from a train window.
list.grammar: Front harmony: Бөгелмә-дә. 'X-да/-дә Y бар' = the there-is frame — reuse it everywhere.

list.known: Southern Tatarstan's grain-and-oil town, grown from a 1911 station on the Volga–Bugulma railway; its sugar refinery is one of the republic's biggest. The name carries нур — 'light'.
list.peg: A beam of light (нур) shining out of a sugar cube on a railway platform.

line.en: In Nurlat there is a sugar factory.

list.grammar: Same 'бар' frame: Нурлат-та (back -та after voiceless т) + izafet шикәр заводы.

knowledge.note: General-knowledge decks for the Hearth's knowledge wing. Each fact: q (question), a (answer), tt (Tatar anchor term), bk (Bashkir comparison per the standing rule), note.

facts.q: When and how did Islam officially arrive on the Volga?
facts.a: In 922, when Ibn Fadlan's embassy from Baghdad reached the Volga Bulgars and Almış Khan adopted Islam.
facts.note: The deep root of Kefeli's 'Becoming Muslim in Imperial Russia' — Islam here predates the Mongol era by three centuries.

facts.q: When did the Kazan Khanate emerge, and from what?
facts.a: Around 1438, founded by Oluğ Möxämmäd out of the breakup of the Golden Horde.
facts.note: The khanate inherited Bulgar's Muslim Volga civilization — the frame of Ross's 'Tatar Empire'.

facts.q: What happened in 1552?
facts.a: Ivan the Terrible's armies took Kazan; the khanate fell and the Volga became a Russian river.
facts.note: Söyembikä and Qol Şärif both enter legend in this siege.

facts.q: Who was Söyembikä?
facts.a: The last ruling queen (regent) of the Kazan Khanate — the leaning tower in the Kremlin bears her name; legend says she leapt from it.
facts.bk: Bashkir folklore claims her too — she was a Nogai princess born near today's Bashkortostan.

facts.q: Who was Qol Şärif?
facts.a: The imam and poet who led Kazan's last defense in 1552. The mosque named for him — the largest in Kazan — was rebuilt in 2005 for the city's millennium.

facts.q: What status does the Kazan Kremlin hold?
facts.a: UNESCO World Heritage Site (2000) — the only surviving Tatar fortress with both a mosque and a cathedral inside.

facts.q: When was the Tatar autonomous republic formed?
facts.a: 1920 (TASSR); today's Republic of Tatarstan declared sovereignty within Russia in 1990.

facts.q: Who is the Tatar national poet?
facts.a: Ğabdulla Tuqay (1886–1913) — died at 27, having rebuilt Tatar poetry in the people's language.
facts.bk: Bashkirs answer with Mäjit Ğafuri — the two literatures grew up as sisters.

facts.q: What is «Туган тел»?
facts.a: Tuqay's poem 'Native Tongue' — sung as the unofficial anthem of the Tatar language.
facts.tt: И туган тел, и матур тел…
facts.note: Тел — халыкның хәтере: language is a people's memory (the Khismatova lecture's thesis in one line).

facts.q: Who wrote the Moabit Notebooks?
facts.a: Musa Cälil — poems written in a Berlin prison before his execution in 1944; posthumously Hero of the Soviet Union.

facts.q: What is the oldest monument of Volga Turkic literature?
facts.a: Qol Ğäli's «Кыйссаи Йосыф» (The Tale of Yusuf), c. 1233 — copied by hand for seven centuries.

facts.q: Which Russian writer studied at Kazan University?
facts.a: Lev Tolstoy (he dropped out); Lenin was expelled from it. The university anchors Kazan's civic wing.

facts.q: What is Sabantuy?
facts.a: The 'plow festival' (сабан + туй) each June — horse races, көрәш belt wrestling, egg-in-spoon races.
facts.bk: Bashkirs celebrate һабантуй — the famous с→һ shift on the festival's own name.

facts.q: What is çäk-çäk?
facts.a: Fried honey-dough pastry — the wedding and welcome sweet. Guests are met with чәк-чәк the way Russians offer bread and salt.

facts.q: Kalfak and tübätäy — what are they?
facts.a: The women's embroidered cap (калфак) and the men's skullcap (түбәтәй) — the two silhouettes of Tatar dress.
facts.note: The калфак is also your memory peg for калу at Söyembikä Tower.

facts.q: What scripts has Tatar been written in?
facts.a: Arabic script for a millennium → Latin Yañalif (1927) → Cyrillic (1939). A 1999 Latin revival was blocked by federal law.

facts.q: What family does Tatar belong to?
facts.a: Kipchak branch of the Turkic family — closest sibling: Bashkir.
facts.bk: башҡорт теле — same sentence patterns, four regular consonant shifts.

facts.q: What did Kazan celebrate in 2005?
facts.a: Its official millennium — 1000 years since the Bulgar-era founding; Kul Sharif mosque reopened for it.

facts.q: What is Ufa called in Bashkir, and where does it stand?
facts.a: Өфө — capital of Bashkortostan, above the Belaya river, which Bashkirs call Ағиҙел ('White Idel').
facts.bk: Идел runs through both homelands: Volga = Идел, Belaya = Ағиҙел.

facts.q: Who is Salavat Yulaev?
facts.a: Poet-warrior (1754–1800) who led Bashkir forces in the Pugachev rebellion; national hero of Bashkortostan.
facts.note: His Ufa monument (1967) is the largest equestrian statue in Russia — ат (horse) is identical in Tatar and Bashkir, and it's the peg at the Salavat station.

facts.q: What is Bizhbulyak?
facts.a: A village and district in southwest Bashkortostan — the innermost ring of this palace, the family hearth.

facts.q: What instrument stands for Bashkir music?
facts.a: The курай — a long open reed flute; the quray flower is on the Bashkortostan flag.

facts.q: What are the four regular Tatar↔Bashkir consonant shifts?
facts.a: с↔һ (сау→һау), ч↔с (өчпочмак→өсмөйөш), к↔ҡ (юк→юҡ), з↔ҙ (бардым→барҙым).
facts.bk: The standing rule: whenever Bashkir appears, find the Tatar cognate through these shifts.

facts.q: How do Tatar and Bashkir infinitives differ?
facts.a: Bashkir adds -ыу/-еү where Tatar has -у/-ү: бару → барыу, килү → килеү.

facts.q: What does the name Sterlitamak literally mean?
facts.a: 'Mouth of the Sterlya' — Стәрле (the river) + тамак (throat/mouth), where the Sterlya meets the Ashkadar near the Belaya.
facts.bk: Стәрлетамаҡ — and тамак is also your throat: Тамагым авырта = my throat hurts.

facts.q: What are the shikhans near Sterlitamak?
facts.a: Lone mountains — Торатау, Юрактау, Куштау — remains of a Permian coral reef ~280 million years old; in 2020 protesters saved Kushtau from soda quarrying.
facts.note: тау = mountain; a fourth, Шахтау, was already quarried away for soda.

facts.q: What is Russia's youngest town?
facts.a: Innopolis, Tatarstan — founded in 2012, inaugurated as a city in 2015 — an IT town around Innopolis University, with delivery robots and driverless taxis.

facts.q: What is Chistopol called in Tatar, and on which river does it stand?
facts.a: Чистай, on the Kama — Чулман in Tatar. It grew rich as a 19th-century grain port.

facts.q: Why do writers and watch collectors both know Chistopol?
facts.a: In WWII the Soviet writers' union (Pasternak among them) was evacuated there, and so was the Moscow watch works that became Vostok — maker of the Командирские.

facts.q: Which famous writer once helped run Bugulma?
facts.a: Jaroslav Hašek, author of 'The Good Soldier Švejk', served as deputy commandant of Bugulma in 1918 and wrote comic tales about it; the town has a Hašek museum.

facts.q: What made Bugulma matter in the 20th century?
facts.a: The railway junction and then oil: the surrounding uplands are Tatarstan's oil country, and Tatneft's research institute (TatNIPIneft) is based there.

facts.q: How did Nurlat begin, and what does its name carry?
facts.a: It grew from a station on the Volga–Bugulma railway (1911) into a sugar-and-oil town; the name carries нур — 'light'.

facts.q: Definite future — 'I will go' (бару)?
facts.a: vstem + -ачак/-әчәк (vowel stems -ячак/-ячәк) + person: барачакмын, сөйләшәчәкмен.

facts.q: Conditional — 'if I come' (килү)?
facts.a: stem + -са/-сә + past-type endings: килсәм, барсаң, эшләсәк.

facts.q: Yes/no question?
facts.a: Particle -мы/-ме on the word being asked: Киләсеңме? Биштәме? Татарчамы?

facts.q: Negating a noun or adjective predicate ('it's not Amir')?
facts.a: түгел after it: Әмир түгел, кыйммәт түгел. Verbs negate inside instead: бармыйм, килмәде.

facts.q: 'I have / I don't have'?
facts.a: Possessive + бар/юк: Минем машинам бар. Акчам юк. No verb 'to have' exists.

facts.q: The six cases and their jobs?
facts.a: Nominative subject · genitive -ның possession · dative -га motion-to · accusative -ны definite object · locative -да place · ablative -дан source.

facts.q: Possessives 'my / your / his-her' on китап?
facts.a: китабым, китабың, китабы — and note voicing: п→б between vowels (китап → китабым).

facts.q: Comparative and superlative?
facts.a: -рак/-рәк with ablative for 'than': Казан Уфадан зуррак; superlative with иң: иң матур шәһәр.

facts.q: 'Need to' pattern?
facts.a: Infinitive + кирәк: барырга кирәк = need to go; negative кирәкми / кирәк түгел.

facts.q: 'Want to' (desiderative)?
facts.a: -асы/-әсе + possessive + килә: эшлисем килә = I want to work; барасым килми = I don't want to go.

facts.q: 'Even if' (concessive)?
facts.a: Conditional + да/дә: кар яуса да = even if it snows; онытсаң да = even if you forget.

facts.q: Plural suffix and its nasal variant?
facts.a: -лар/-ләр, but after м/н/ң it becomes -нар/-нәр: урамнар, көннәр, таулар.

facts.q: Compound verbs — which part conjugates?
facts.a: Only the final verb; the first word is frozen: йоклап китәчәкмен, ял иттем, сатып алдым.

facts.q: 'At N o'clock'?
facts.a: сәгать + numeral + locative: сәгать биштә = at five; сәгать ничәдә? = at what time?

facts.q: Softening a claim to 'probably'?
facts.a: -дыр/-дер on the predicate (киләдер = probably coming, өйдәдер = probably home) or adverbs бәлки, ихтимал, мөгаен.

facts.q: The three place questions and their cases?
facts.a: кайда (at → -да), кая (to → -га), кайдан (from → -дан) — question and answer always share the case.
